text: How would you describe the current economic situation in Germany?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 42% positive / 58% negative. Note: initial estimate had this at 16% positive — actual data shows more optimism. AfD voters and East Germans skew heavily to 'Very bad'. Strong economic pessimism signal despite 42% somewhat good overall.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable view of Russia?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 86.8% unfavorable — post-Ukraine invasion sentiment. This is the strongest partisan split in 2024 Germany: AfD and BSW voters are disproportionately favorable toward Russia. Greens/SPD/CDU voters cluster in unfavorable. Key test of AfD and East German persona calibration.

text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in Germany?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 59.6% satisfied. AfD voters near-unanimously dissatisfied (C/D). East German personas should show lower satisfaction than West German counterparts of equal party lean — the East/West WorldviewAnchor gap test.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable view of the European Union?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 71.1% favorable. Germany historically pro-EU. AfD voters heavily unfavorable. Smallest partisan gap between CDU/SPD/Greens/FDP of all 15 questions — all mainstream parties favor EU. Important ceiling test.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable view of China?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 81.3% unfavorable. Higher unfavorability than EU average. FDP/business-aligned personas create a 'somewhat favorable' floor (trade dependency). AfD voters split — some align with Russia/China axis, others with anti-China Western stance.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable view of NATO?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 72.8% favorable. Strong security/defense signal. AfD and BSW voters disproportionately unfavorable (NATO-skeptic, aligned with Russia view in de02). CDU/CSU and Greens most favorable. Complements the Russia question as a foreign policy pair.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Alternative for Germany (AfD)?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 15.8% favorable, 84.2% unfavorable. Most polarising domestic question — AfD voters are (by definition) in the favorable minority. Tests whether personas correctly resist group-consistency pressure: non-AfD personas should almost all be C or D.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Christian Democratic Union (CDU)?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 49.5% favorable — the most evenly split of the party questions. Centre-right/mainstream signal. CDU voters overwhelmingly favorable; AfD voters split (CDU as establishment rival); Greens/SPD lean unfavorable. Tests centrist calibration.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of The Greens/Alliance 90?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 41.7% favorable — the most evenly split party. Change tolerance signal: highly polarising across age and region. AfD and BSW voters strongly unfavorable. East German personas more unfavorable than West German equivalents. Young urban West Germans disproportionately favorable.

text: How important is religion in your life?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 37.1% not at all important — reflects 42% religiously unaffiliated population. Bavarian Catholic personas should cluster in A/B. East German personas cluster in D (GDR atheist legacy). Turkish-origin Muslim personas in A/B. Critical East/West religious calibration test.

text: How important is it to be Christian to be truly German?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 63% say being Christian is NOT AT ALL important to being truly German — Germany's secular national identity. Older, Catholic Bavaria personas most likely to say important (A/B). Turkish-origin personas the key test: should answer C/D as German-identified Muslims. Tests Moral Foundationalism and national identity dimensions.

text: How much confidence do you have in former U.S. President Donald Trump to do the right thing regarding world affairs?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 86.7% no/little confidence — one of highest Trump disapproval rates globally. AfD voters are the outlier: pro-Trump alignment is a defining AfD-voter signal in 2024. Greens voters near-unanimous D. Institutional trust dimension: skepticism about US leadership post-Trump.

text: When children today in Germany grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 3-option question (A/B/C only — no 4-way split in this survey). 61.7% worse off — among Europe's most pessimistic. 2024 recession + deindustrialisation fears. East German personas more pessimistic. AfD voters most pessimistic. Note: economic pessimism correlates with but is not identical to political lean.

text: How big a problem is the gap between the rich and the poor in Germany?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 90.5% see inequality as a big problem — near-universal concern. Tests Individualism dimension: FDP personas and upper-class personas should cluster in C/D; Left/SPD/BSW personas in A. East Germans disproportionately in A. One of the flattest partisan distributions but strongest class/education signal.

text: Do you think Germany's economic system needs to be reformed?
source: Pew Research Global Attitudes, Spring 2024 (Germany N=1,008)
notes: VERIFIED from raw data. 63.1% say major/complete reform needed. Change Tolerance + Individualism interaction: FDP personas lean toward minor changes (market self-correction); Left/BSW/AfD personas lean toward complete reform (for different reasons — redistributive vs nativist). Good test of construct independence: left and right reformers have opposite visions.
